1: Behold, the LORD's hand is not shortened, that it cannot save, or his ear dull, that it cannot hear. The not-shortened arm of YHWH — the arm that is powerful enough to save. Isaiah 53:1 asks: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? — and answers with the Servant's suffering. The arm that appears shortened and defeated at the cross is the arm of YHWH acting in its fullest capacity: Hebrews 2:14-15 — Christ destroyed the one who has the power of death through death. What looks like the arm failing is the arm reaching all the way into death to bring out the captives. Acts 2:24: God raised Jesus, loosing the pangs of death, because it was not possible for him to be held by it.
2: But your iniquities have made a separation between you and your God, and your sins have hidden his face from you so that he does not hear. The fundamental problem of human existence: sin as separation. This is the diagnosis that makes the gospel necessary. Romans 5:10: while we were enemies we were reconciled to God by the death of his Son. 2 Corinthians 5:18-19: all this is from God, who through Christ reconciled us to himself... in Christ God was reconciling the world to himself, not counting their trespasses against them. The separation of Isaiah 59:2 is precisely what Ephesians 2:13-16 says has been overcome: those who were far off have been brought near by the blood of Christ who is our peace.
3: For your hands are defiled with blood and your fingers with iniquity; your lips have spoken lies; your tongue mutters wickedness. The comprehensive indictment of the sinning body — hands, fingers, lips, tongue — every instrument of human action corrupted. Paul's sin-catena in Romans 3:10-18 weaves together texts from Psalms and Isaiah precisely because Isaiah 59 provides a full-body inventory of human depravity. The same Spirit who reveals this indictment provides the remedy through Christ: Titus 3:5 — he saved us, not because of works done by us in righteousness, but according to his own mercy, by the washing of regeneration and renewing of the Holy Spirit.
4: No one enters suit justly; no one goes to law honestly; they rely on empty pleas, they speak lies, they conceive mischief and give birth to iniquity. The corrupt legal system — a sign of total social breakdown. This is the context for Paul's charge in Romans 3:10-18 that none is righteous, no, not one. John 5:22: the Father judges no one, but has given all judgment to the Son. The legal corruption of Isaiah 59:4 makes necessary the appointment of the Son as the one true just judge — the one in whose court no empty plea succeeds and no false testimony stands.
5: They hatch adders' eggs; they weave the spider's web; he who eats their eggs dies, and from one that is crushed a viper is hatched. The poisonous work of the wicked — eggs that kill, webs that trap. Matthew 3:7; Isaiah 23:33: Jesus calls the Pharisees and Sadducees a brood of vipers — using the serpent-imagery of Isaiah 59:5. Luke 10:19: Jesus gives the disciples authority to tread on serpents and scorpions — the reversal of the viper-web trap. The work of Christ is the crushing of the serpent's head (Genesis 3:15) — the one who destroys the egg-layers and dismantles the web.
6: Their webs will not serve as clothing; men will not cover themselves with what they make. Their works are works of iniquity, and deeds of violence are in their hands. The self-made covering that fails — human attempts to clothe themselves with their own works. Genesis 3:7: Adam and Eve made fig-leaf coverings — the first human attempt at self-cover after the fall. Genesis 3:21: YHWH made garments of skin — the first divine provision of covering through sacrifice. Christ is the true garment: Romans 13:14 — put on the Lord Jesus Christ. Galatians 3:27: as many of you as were baptized into Christ have put on Christ. The failed webs of human self-righteousness are replaced by the robe of Christ's righteousness.
7: Their feet run to evil, and they are swift to shed innocent blood; their thoughts are thoughts of iniquity; desolation and destruction are in their highways. Romans 3:15-17 quotes this verse directly as part of the comprehensive OT indictment of universal human sinfulness: their feet are swift to shed blood; in their paths are ruin and misery, and the way of peace they have not known. Paul uses Isaiah 59:7-8 as the culminating evidence in his argument that all — Jew and Gentile — are under sin and need the righteousness that comes through faith in Christ (Romans 3:21-22). The feet swift to shed blood include those that carried Jesus to Calvary.
8: The way of peace they have not known, and there is no justice in their paths; they have made their roads crooked; no one who treads on them knows peace. Romans 3:17 cites this as the final word in Paul's universal-sin indictment: the way of peace they have not known. Ephesians 2:14-17: he himself is our peace, who has made us both one and has broken down in his flesh the dividing wall of hostility... and he came and preached peace to you who were far off and peace to those who were near. Christ is the way of peace that Isaiah 59:8 says they have not known — the one who makes crooked roads straight (Isaiah 40:4) and who is himself the Way (John 14:6).
9: Therefore justice is far from us, and righteousness does not overtake us; we hope for light, and behold, darkness, and for brightness, but we walk in gloom. The communal lament of those who expected righteousness and received darkness. Matthew 4:16 (citing Isaiah 9:2): the people dwelling in darkness have seen a great light; and for those dwelling in the region and shadow of death, on them a light has dawned. John 1:4-5: in him was life, and the life was the light of men. The light shines in the darkness, and the darkness has not overcome it. The one who comes into the community groping in darkness (vv9-10) is the Light of the world whose arrival Isaiah 59 anticipates.
10: We grope for the wall like the blind; we grope like those who have no eyes; we stumble at noon as in the twilight, among those in full vigor we are like dead men. The blindness-at-noon image — the darkness so total that even daylight gives no help. John 9 is the enacted Isaiah 59:10: Jesus heals the man born blind and then declares I am the light of the world (John 9:5). The Pharisees who can physically see are spiritually blind (John 9:39-41: for judgment I came into this world, that those who do not see may see, and those who see may become blind). The stumbling-at-noon is the human condition that Christ's coming light addresses.
11: We all growl like bears; we moan and moan like doves; we hope for justice, but there is none; for salvation, but it is far from us. The animal-sounds of lament — the failure of hope deferred. Hebrews 11:13: these all died in faith, not having received the things promised, but having seen them and greeted them from afar. The people of Isaiah 59:11 moan for salvation that is far off — and then it arrives in the person of Jesus. Luke 2:25-32: Simeon, who had hoped to see the Lord's salvation, holds the infant Christ and says my eyes have seen your salvation. The far-off salvation comes near in the incarnation.
12: For our transgressions are multiplied before you, and our sins testify against us; for our transgressions are with us, and we know our iniquities. The honest communal confession — sins that testify against us. Luke 18:13: the tax collector's prayer — God, be merciful to me, a sinner! — is the model response to this condition. 1 John 1:9: if we confess our sins, he is faithful and just to forgive us our sins and to cleanse us from all unrighteousness. The confession of Isaiah 59:12 is the necessary posture that receives the forgiveness Christ provides. Romans 10:9-10: with the heart one believes and is justified, and with the mouth one confesses and is saved.
13: Transgressing, and denying the LORD, and turning back from following our God, speaking oppression and revolt, conceiving and uttering from the heart lying words. The full description of covenant apostasy — turning from YHWH while harboring deceit in the heart. John 8:44: Jesus tells the opponents: you are of your father the devil... there is no truth in him. When he lies, he speaks out of his own character, for he is a liar and the father of lies. The lying words conceived in the heart (Isaiah 59:13) trace back to the father of lies. Christ is the counter-word: John 1:14 — the Word became flesh, full of grace and truth. Truth embodied confronts the lying words conceived in the heart.
14: Justice is turned back, and righteousness stands far away; for truth has stumbled in the public squares, and uprightness cannot enter. Truth stumbling in the public square — the ultimate portrait of systemic covenant breakdown. John 18:38: Pilate asks what is truth? — the Roman governor unable to recognize truth when Truth himself stands before him (John 14:6). The trial of Jesus is Isaiah 59:14 enacted in history: justice is turned back (the acquitted is crucified), righteousness stands far away (Barabbas is released), truth stumbles (perjured testimony accepted), uprightness cannot enter (the innocent is condemned). The cross is the supreme instance of justice inverted — and simultaneously YHWH's plan for the supreme justice to be accomplished through it.
15: Truth is lacking, and he who departs from evil makes himself a prey. The LORD saw it, and it displeased him that there was no justice. The divine displeasure at the absence of justice — YHWH sees and is moved to act. Hebrews 4:13: no creature is hidden from his sight, but all are naked and exposed to the eyes of him to whom we must give account. The one who sees all corruption and is displeased is the Father who sends the Son to address what cannot be addressed from within the human system. The cross is the divine response to divine displeasure at injustice — not by ignoring it but by absorbing it.
16: He saw that there was no man, and wondered that there was no one to intercede; then his own arm brought him salvation, and his righteousness upheld him. No human intercessor — so YHWH's own arm acts. Isaiah 53:1: to whom has the arm of the LORD been revealed? The arm that saves when no human can is the arm of the Servant — Christ who is both the divine arm and the human intercessor. Hebrews 7:25: Christ always lives to make intercession for those who draw near to God through him. 1 Timothy 2:5: there is one mediator between God and men, the man Christ Jesus. When there was no human intercessor, YHWH himself became one — by taking on flesh.
17: He put on righteousness as a breastplate, and a helmet of salvation on his head; he put on garments of vengeance for clothing, and wrapped himself in zeal as a cloak. The divine warrior's armor. Ephesians 6:14-17 distributes this imagery to the believer: the breastplate of righteousness... the helmet of salvation... the sword of the Spirit. The armor that YHWH wears in Isaiah 59 becomes the armor he gives to those in Christ — because Christ wore it first. 1 Thessalonians 5:8: putting on the breastplate of faith and love, and for a helmet the hope of salvation. The believer's armor is derivative: they are equipped with what the divine warrior wore when he came to save.
18: According to their deeds, so will he repay, wrath to his adversaries, repayment to his enemies; to the coastlands he will render repayment. The eschatological repayment to all who opposed the covenant. Revelation 22:12: Behold, I am coming soon, bringing my recompense with me, to repay each one for what he has done. The returning Christ is the executor of the judgment Isaiah 59:18 announces. The scope — to the coastlands (Gentile nations) — is the global scope of the final judgment when every nation stands before the Son of Man (Matthew 25:31-32). The wrath of YHWH absorbed at the cross by Christ is the same wrath that returns at the parousia for those who rejected him.
19: So they shall fear the name of the LORD from the west, and his glory from the rising of the sun; for he will come like a rushing stream, the Spirit of the LORD driving it. The universal east-to-west proclamation of YHWH's glory, driven by the Spirit. Acts 2:2-4: at Pentecost, suddenly there came from heaven a sound like a mighty rushing wind, and it filled the entire house where they were sitting. The Spirit as a rushing stream driving the proclamation of YHWH's glory from Jerusalem (Pentecost) to the ends of the earth (Acts 1:8). John 3:8: the wind [Spirit] blows where it wishes, and you hear its sound, but you do not know where it comes from or where it goes.
20: And a Redeemer will come to Zion, to those in Jacob who turn from transgression, declares the LORD. Romans 11:26-27 quotes this verse at the climax of Paul's argument about Israel's eschatological salvation: and in this way all Israel will be saved, as it is written, The Deliverer will come from Zion, he will banish ungodliness from Jacob. The Redeemer who comes from Zion is Christ — and Paul's use of Isaiah 59:20 grounds his confidence that the partial hardening of Israel is not permanent: the Deliverer will come again, and at his coming, the remnant of Israel who turn from transgression will be saved. Isaiah 59:20 is thus both a prophecy of the first coming and the second.
21: And as for me, this is my covenant with them, says the LORD: my Spirit that is upon you, and my words that I have put in your mouth, shall not depart out of your mouth, or out of the mouth of your offspring, or out of your children's offspring, says the LORD, from this time forth and forevermore. The Spirit-and-word new covenant — permanently placed on the community and their children. Acts 2:16-18 (citing Joel 2): the Pentecostal Spirit-outpouring is the fulfillment. Acts 2:39: the promise is for you and for your children and for all who are far off. Ephesians 6:17: the sword of the Spirit, which is the word of God. The Spirit and the word placed permanently on the covenant community are the new covenant gift that Christ secures and pours out — the Spirit he sends (John 16:7) and the word he is (John 1:1).
